description: Detailed answers for Java interview questions 321-352 covering Stream API, Functional Interfaces, and advanced Java concepts. Each answer: 200-400 words with Java code examples and Mermaid diagrams for flow/architecture concepts.
mermaid_note: Mermaid diagrams included where applicable for concepts involving flow (thread lifecycle), architecture (GC phases), or complex sequences.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why can a Stream be consumed only once? What would happen if we try to reuse the same stream?
answers.answer: A Java Stream is a lazily-evaluated pipeline that can only be consumed once. The reason: streams are designed for single-pass traversal of data elements, not repeated access. Once a terminal operation (collect, forEach, count) is invoked, the stream is exhausted and cannot be reused. Attempting reuse throws IllegalStateException. This design enforces functional principles where each stream instance represents a specific computation pipeline. The single-consumption rule is enforced via an internal flag set when terminal operation begins. This restriction prevents accidental reuse bugs and forces functional thinking about data processing. If you need to process same data multiple times, create new streams or collect into a reusable collection. The stream contract establishes that operations build up lazily without executing until terminal operation triggers evaluation. Once triggered, internal state becomes invalid. This is crucial for memory efficiency with large datasets—streams don't buffer all intermediate results. Attempting to reuse streams violates their contract and represents a coding error that should be caught early.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why do we need Stream if we can write code without stream API?
answers.answer: Stream API provides functional-style operations making code more readable, maintainable, and performant. Streams enable declarative programming—specify WHAT not HOW. They support lazy evaluation—intermediate operations don't execute until terminal operation called, enabling optimizations like short-circuit evaluation in findFirst(). Streams provide built-in parallelization—convert sequential to parallel with single .parallel() call without managing thread pools. They integrate seamlessly with functional paradigm through lambda expressions and method references. Streams handle boilerplate code implicitly (null checks, iteration logic). They encourage immutability reducing side effects, making code safer in concurrent environments. For large datasets with complex transformations, streams are significantly more efficient due to internal optimizations and short-circuiting. Stream operations are composable, allowing complex data processing pipelines expressed clearly and concisely. The functional approach reduces bugs compared to imperative loops with mutable variables and complex control flow.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can you explain Stream Pipeline Structure?
answers.answer: A Stream pipeline has three components: SOURCE (collection, array, generator), INTERMEDIATE OPERATIONS (filter, map, flatMap—lazy transformations returning Stream), and TERMINAL OPERATION (collect, forEach, reduce—consumes stream). Key insight: intermediate operations are lazy—they build transformation spec without processing elements. Only terminal operation triggers execution. Pipeline executes in single pass, applying all transformations to each element before moving to next. This lazy evaluation enables optimizations like short-circuit evaluation and parallel processing. Stream doesn't support random access like collections; it's a one-time traversal pipeline. The pipeline concept separates what transformations to apply (intermediates) from when to apply them (terminal operation). This separation enables the JVM to optimize the entire pipeline as a unit rather than making separate passes through data.
answers.code_example:
Stream<Integer> pipeline = numbers.stream()     // SOURCE
    .filter(n -> n > 5)                      // INTERMEDIATE 1
    .map(n -> n * 2)                         // INTERMEDIATE 2
    .distinct()                              // INTERMEDIATE 3
    .limit(10)                               // INTERMEDIATE 4
    .collect(Collectors.toList());            // TERMINAL (triggers execution)

// Lazy evaluation demonstrated
Stream<String> words = Arrays.stream(new String[]{"hello", "world", "java"});
Stream<Integer> lengths = words
    .peek(w -> System.out.println("Peek: " + w))  // Doesn't execute yet
    .map(String::length)                           // Doesn't execute yet
    .peek(len -> System.out.println("Length: " + len));
// Nothing printed yet! Now execute:
lengths.forEach(System.out::println);  // TERMINAL - everything executes

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why are intermediate operations called lazy?
answers.answer: Intermediate operations don't immediately process data when invoked. They merely register transformations, building a pipeline that executes when terminal operation triggers it. This design enables short-circuit evaluation—findFirst() stops after finding first match, limit(n) processes only n elements. Lazy evaluation improves performance with large datasets: filtering 1 million elements then applying limit(5) processes only until 5 items found, not all 1 million. It enables operation fusion optimization—multiple operations combined into single pass rather than multiple traversals. Lazy evaluation allows composition of complex pipelines where each operation optimizes independently. This contrasts with imperative code creating temporary intermediate collections, requiring multiple passes through data. The term 'lazy' means 'deferred execution'—pipeline is built but not evaluated until terminal operation activates it.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: If a stream pipeline contains multiple filter() operations, does it iterate the collection multiple times?
answers.answer: No, multiple filter() operations cause SINGLE iteration through collection. JVM internally fuses operations into composite function. Each element flows through all filters before next element is processed. This is 'operation fusion' or 'stream fusion'—one of key optimizations. Each element traverses entire pipeline in single pass. No intermediate collections created (unless explicitly collected). Short-circuit operations stop processing when result found. This behavior radically differs from imperative code creating temporary filtered lists requiring multiple iterations. Stream fusion means: each element processed exactly once through all operations, no intermediate collections, optimized execution path. This efficiency is why streams often outperform traditional loops with multiple conditions.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: You are processing 10 million records using Streams. Performance becomes slow. What factors should you check?
answers.answer: Examine OPERATION COMPLEXITY—expensive operations (database queries, file I/O, complex calculations) executed 10M times. Check PARALLEL STREAM USAGE—parallelization has overhead; for small operations or I/O-bound tasks, sequential faster. Investigate INTERMEDIATE COLLECTION CREATION—creating objects for each element causes GC pressure with 10M objects. Check LAZY EVALUATION MISUSE—if collecting everything then reprocessing, optimization benefits lost. Look for STATEFUL OPERATIONS—distinct(), sorted() require buffering entire intermediate results. Profile using JFR or JVM profilers to identify actual bottleneck. Often problem isn't streams themselves but how they're used. Database queries in filter lambdas create N+1 problem. Unnecessary object creation per element creates massive GC pressure. Inappropriate parallelization adds overhead exceeding benefits.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: When should Streams be avoided?
answers.answer: Avoid streams for COMPLEX CONTROL FLOW (multiple breaks, complex conditions, nested if-else, early returns). Streams excel at linear transformations but struggle with branching logic. Avoid when PRIMARY GOAL is SIDE EFFECTS—forEach() on loop is more honest about intent. Avoid MULTI-LEVEL NESTING—deeply nested flatMap() chains harder to read than nested loops. Avoid when operations DEPEND ON STATE across iterations. Avoid PERFORMANCE-CRITICAL INNER LOOPS—slight overhead vs optimized imperative code (though JIT typically mitigates). Consider TEAM FAMILIARITY—if team uncomfortable with functional programming, clarity suffers. Rule of thumb: if explaining WHAT code does is harder than explaining HOW, use streams. If explaining HOW is harder, use loops. Readability and maintainability trump using latest API features.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can Streams modify the original collection?
answers.answer: Streams do NOT modify original collection. They're designed for functional-style transformations producing new collections or values. Immutability principle prevents concurrent modification exceptions. All intermediate operations return new Stream objects without altering source. Terminal operations like collect() create new collections, not modify original. However, if working with mutable objects, you CAN modify objects themselves but not collection structure. To modify original collection: use replaceAll() or removeIf(), or traditional loops. Stream design prevents accidental modifications, makes streams thread-safe by design, and encourages functional thinking where transformations have no side effects. Immutability contract allows safe stream usage in concurrent environments without worrying about concurrent modification, provided source isn't modified while stream processes.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Difference between map() and flatMap()? When prefer flatMap()?
answers.answer: map() applies function to each element returning transformed results. flatMap() applies function returning Stream for each element, then flattens into single stream. Conceptually: map(function) equals flatMap(element -> Stream.of(function(element))). Use map() when input maps to exactly one output. Use flatMap() when input maps to zero, one, or multiple outputs, or transformation returns Stream/Collection. flatMap() replaces nested for-loops. Common cases: converting list of lists into single flat list, extracting multiple values per element (customer has multiple addresses), expanding strings into characters, processing nested data structures. The name combines 'map' (transform) and 'flat' (flatten). Single flatMap() replacing what would be nested loops with intermediate collection creation.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why is forEach() terminal but peek() intermediate?
answers.answer: forEach() is terminal—returns void, consumes entire stream, performs final actions. peek() is intermediate—returns Stream, allows chaining. Key distinction: intent and return type. forEach() signals 'processing ends here, perform final actions'. peek() signals 'inspect element while processing, continue pipeline'. Using peek() for primary side effects is anti-pattern—violates functional principles, obscures intent. Streams designed for data transformation, not side effects. If primary goal is side effects, forEach() makes explicit. peek() meant for debugging, not production side effects. Stream documentation warns against peek() for anything except debugging. Good rule: production code using peek() probably should use forEach() or traditional loops instead.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why is Stream not a data structure?
answers.answer: Stream is NOT a data structure—it doesn't store data. It's a pipeline representing operations applied to element source. Streams process sequentially, consumed once. Elements computed on-the-fly, not pre-stored. Infinite generators (Stream.generate()) create elements as needed. Implications: memory-efficient for large datasets (no simultaneous holding of all data), lazy-evaluated operations, single-use, supports functional composition without intermediate collections, no random access (not indexed). To store processed results, explicitly call collect() materializing stream into List, Set, Map, or collection. This explicit materialization clarifies memory implications and encourages functional thinking.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: What happens if source collection modified during stream processing?
answers.answer: Modifying source collection during stream processing results in unpredictable behavior or ConcurrentModificationException. Stream expects collection stability during iteration. Modifications cause either exception detection or silently inconsistent results. Results are unpredictable—elements added might not be included, removed elements might skip other elements. Parallel streams make it even more dangerous—concurrent modification leads to data corruption. Safest practice: never modify source collection during stream processing. If modifications needed, do so after stream completes (after terminal operation). If absolutely must modify during processing, understand risks and test thoroughly. Better approach: create new collection with transformations applied rather than modifying source.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can Stream pipeline run without terminal operation?
answers.answer: No, Stream pipeline produces no results without terminal operation. Intermediate operations alone define transformations but execute nothing. Pipeline remains dormant until terminal operation invokes it. filter(), map(), flatMap() etc. construct transformation specification. Nothing processes, no functions called, no data flows until terminal operation like collect(), forEach(), count(), reduce(), findFirst() called. Stream is lazy—nothing executes until activation. IDEs warn about missing terminal operation. Processing doesn't happen until terminal operation invoked. This lazy evaluation principle enables optimizations like short-circuiting, parallel processing, memory efficiency.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: What is Parallel Stream? How does Java decide thread count?
answers.answer: Parallel Stream processes elements concurrently using multiple threads via ForkJoinPool. Divides source into segments, processes multiple simultaneously across processor cores. Java decides thread count via ForkJoinPool.commonPool() creating threads equal to Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors() - 1 (minimum 1). Default: parallelism = cores - 1. Override via system property: -Djava.util.concurrent.ForkJoinPool.common.parallelism=8. Uses ForkJoinPool internally with work-stealing framework ensuring optimal load balancing. Automatically partitions source via Spliterator—different collections have different splitting strategies (ArrayList splits evenly, LinkedList has poor split properties). Performance gains NOT guaranteed. Often worse than sequential when: operations are simple/fast, data size small, poor split properties, I/O involved (threads block), source infinite/unordered. Best for large datasets (millions elements) with CPU-intensive operations.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: What operations are unsuitable for Parallel Streams?
answers.answer: STATEFUL operations depending on order/previous results (distinct, sorted, limit, skip) need synchronization, negating parallelization benefits. SIDE-EFFECT operations in lambdas cause race conditions and data corruption in concurrent execution. I/O-BOUND operations (database, network, files) cause threads to block waiting, wasting resources and starving pool. SMALL datasets rarely benefit—overhead exceeds computation. ORDERED TERMINAL operations (findFirst) incur overhead maintaining order. UNORDERED sources hard to split (LinkedList). Operations depending on SEQUENTIAL ORDER of previous results. LAZY/INFINITE streams hard to partition. General guidance: parallel streams need millions of elements and CPU-intensive operations to show benefits. Profile to confirm improvements before using parallelization.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Parallel Streams use which thread pool? Can it affect other tasks?
answers.answer: Parallel Streams use ForkJoinPool.commonPool()—global shared thread pool across entire JVM. All parallel streams compete for same threads. If one component saturates pool with long-running parallel stream, other parallel streams elsewhere in application get blocked. Resource contention issue in microservices sharing same JVM. One greedy application component monopolizes threads, degrading others' performance. Blocking operations (I/O, locks) amplify problem—threads blocked can't process other work. Mitigation: create custom ForkJoinPool instances for CPU-intensive operations, keeping separate from common pool. For critical paths needing predictable performance, avoid common pool by using custom pools or ExecutorService.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: If stream operation depends on previous results, use Parallel Stream?
answers.answer: No, avoid parallel streams for operations depending on previous results. Such operations are inherently sequential, violating parallel streams' data independence assumption. Each element's processing requires previous element's result. This strict dependency chain prevents parallelization. Using parallel streams adds overhead without benefit, actually degrading performance due to thread coordination, context switching, synchronization overhead without corresponding benefit. Examples: cumulative sum, dependent filtering, transformations based on previous values, building ordered sequences. Many operations have hidden dependencies on ordering or previous state (limit depends on processing exactly n elements, findFirst maintains ordering). For such operations, use traditional loops or redesign algorithm eliminating dependencies. Sometimes restructure sequential dependencies into parallel-friendly operations using techniques like prefix sums or parallel reduction. Key principle: parallel streams excel at embarrassingly parallel problems where elements process completely independently.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: When prefer sequential streams over parallel streams?
answers.answer: Sequential streams are DEFAULT choice in most scenarios. Parallel should only be considered for specific conditions: large datasets (millions elements) with CPU-intensive operations. Use sequential when: dataset is small, operations are simple/fast, source has poor split characteristics (LinkedList), operations involve I/O, code needs predictable performance, operations have side effects or depend on ordering, running in resource-constrained environments, overhead of threads exceeds computational benefit. Sequential streams are simpler, easier to debug (no race conditions), require no special resource consideration, guarantee predictable execution order. For I/O-bound operations, CompletableFuture or virtual threads better alternatives. Most business logic benefits from sequential streams' simplicity and predictability. Principle: pay for what you use—if unsure parallelization helps, don't use it. Measure actual impact before adding parallelization complexity.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: What makes interface a Functional Interface? Can it have multiple methods?
answers.answer: Functional Interface has exactly ONE abstract method. Can have multiple methods total—additional methods can be default (with implementation) or static (with implementation). @FunctionalInterface annotation (optional) documents intent and triggers compile-time checking. Lambda expressions implement the single abstract method. Default methods and static methods provide implementations, so they don't need to be implemented by lambdas. If interface had multiple abstract methods, compiler wouldn't know which method lambda implements, creating ambiguity. This is why limit to one abstract method. Comparator example: has one abstract method compare() but many defaults like reversed(), thenComparing(). The single abstract method rule applies entire inheritance hierarchy—total distinct abstract methods must remain one. Functional interfaces powerful abstraction combining simplicity (for lambdas) with functionality (defaults/statics).

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why allow multiple default methods but only one abstract method?
answers.answer: Default methods don't create ambiguity for lambda expressions because they're pre-implemented. Lambda implements exactly one abstract method—the behavior specification. Default methods are utility functions complementing functional interface's purpose. Allows functional interfaces to be expressive and feature-rich without breaking lambda contract. Single abstract method rule ensures unambiguous lambda resolution. Default methods don't participate in lambda resolution—they're already implemented. This pragmatic balance enables: simple lambda implementation, rich utility methods, composable operations without cluttering implementing classes. Comparator example: has many defaults (reversed, thenComparing, etc.) providing convenient utilities while maintaining single abstract method compare() for lambda implementation.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can Functional Interface extend another interface? What if both have abstract methods?
answers.answer: Functional interface CAN extend another interface IF combined result is exactly ONE abstract method. If parent has one and child adds another distinct abstract method, total becomes two—no longer functional. If child overrides/refines parent's method without adding new ones, remains functional. Parent interface with zero abstract methods (only defaults/statics) allows child one abstract method. Functional interface hierarchy leaf represents single functional contract. Comparator-like designs exemplify this pattern. @FunctionalInterface annotation prevents accidental violations when refactoring or extending interfaces. Without it, inadvertently adding second abstract method might go undetected until runtime.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can functional interface contain static methods?
answers.answer: Yes, functional interface CAN contain static methods. Static methods don't affect functional interface status because they belong to interface itself, not instances. They're called through interface type, not instances. They have implementations, don't need to be overridden. Static methods don't participate in lambda expression resolution. Common for providing factory methods, utility functions. Multiple static methods allowed while maintaining functional status. Combination of one abstract method, multiple defaults, and multiple statics creates powerful abstraction—simple for lambdas, feature-rich for utilities. Example: Comparator provides static factories like naturalOrder(), comparing(), etc.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Give examples of Functional Interfaces
answers.answer: Core four: Predicate<T> (returns boolean—filtering), Function<T,R> (transforms T to R—mapping), Consumer<T> (performs action on T—side effects), Supplier<T> (produces T without arguments—generation). Specialized variants: IntPredicate, LongFunction, DoubleConsumer for primitives (avoid boxing). Comparator<T> (compare method—sorting). Runnable (run()—executable tasks). Callable<V> (call()—returns value). BinaryOperator<T> (combines two values). UnaryOperator<T> (transforms same type). BiFunction<T,U,R> (two arguments). BiConsumer<T,U> (action on two arguments). Stream API provides numerous additional functional interfaces. Choice depends on specific use case and whether special behavior needed.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Purpose of @FunctionalInterface annotation if optional?
answers.answer: @FunctionalInterface is optional but serves important documentation and compile-time checking. Compiler verifies interface has exactly one abstract method, immediately reporting errors if violated. Without annotation, mistakes go undetected until runtime. Serves as documentation signaling interface designed for lambdas, maintaining single abstract method contract. Prevents common refactoring mistakes—adding second abstract method immediately caught. Best practice: always annotate interfaces for lambdas, even though optional. Defensive programming costing nothing providing significant value through early error detection and clear intent communication.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why does Comparator qualify as functional interface despite many methods?
answers.answer: Comparator qualifies because it has exactly ONE abstract method: compare(T o1, T o2). Other methods are default or static with implementations. When retrofitted to functional interface in Java 8, designers added convenience defaults and statics without additional abstract methods. This allows simple lambda implementation (Comparator<T>) while providing rich utility defaults like reversed(), thenComparing(). Demonstrates powerful design pattern: simple interface for lambdas combined with functional methods for expressiveness. Comparator shows functional interface concept doesn't mean interfaces must be simple—it means one abstract method defining primary contract. Additional methods enhance usability without breaking lambda implementation.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Difference between Predicate, Function, Consumer, and Supplier?
answers.answer: PREDICATE<T> takes value, returns boolean—answers yes/no question about value (filter uses Predicate). FUNCTION<T,R> takes T, returns R—transforms input to output (map uses Function). CONSUMER<T> takes value, returns void—performs action for side effects (forEach uses Consumer). SUPPLIER<T> takes nothing, returns T—produces values on demand (generate uses Supplier). Conceptually differ in transformation pattern. Predicate questions value, Function transforms data, Consumer acts on data, Supplier generates data. In functional terms: Predicate is unary predicate function, Function is unary function, Consumer applies function for side effects, Supplier is nullary (zero-arg) function. Understanding distinctions helps choose right tool and makes code more readable.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: How does compiler determine which method lambda expression refers to?
answers.answer: Compiler uses 'target type inference' matching lambda signature with functional interface's abstract method. Target type determined by context: assignment target, method parameter type, or return type. When assigning to Predicate<String>, compiler infers abstract method is test(String). Compiler verifies lambda signature matches: parameters compatible with method parameters, return type compatible with method return type, lambda able to throw declared checked exceptions. For method arguments, compiler uses declared parameter type. In overloads, uses overload resolution rules for best match. Compiler sophisticated inferring generics, diamond operators, complex overloads. If ambiguity exists, must explicitly cast or specify types. Lambda signature must be compatible but not identical—type widening and compatibility rules apply.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why must variables in lambdas be final or effectively final?
answers.answer: Variables captured by lambdas must be final or effectively final due to memory model and thread safety. Local variables exist on stack, can go out of scope. Lambda needs stable, unchanging variable access. Variable is 'effectively final' if never reassigned post-initialization. If lambda captured mutable variable changing after creation, lambda behavior would be unpredictable depending on execution timing. This violates functional programming where functions should be deterministic. Lambdas often used concurrently (parallel streams, async). Without effectively final requirement, thread-safety issues arise—multiple threads racing to read/modify captured variable causing data corruption. Effectively final requirement prevents subtle concurrency bugs. Implementation captures variable VALUE (primitives) or reference (objects), not mutable binding. Once captured, value cannot change. For mutable state, use mutable objects (AtomicInteger) or instance variables. Restriction applies only to local variables, not instance or class variables, which can modify freely in lambdas.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Difference between lambda expressions and anonymous class?
answers.answer: Lambdas are Java 8+ syntactic sugar for functional interfaces (one abstract method). Anonymous classes older, general-purpose, implement any interface or extend classes. Lambdas have cleaner syntax. Lambda 'this' refers to enclosing class; anonymous classes create own scope. Lambdas work only with functional interfaces; anonymous classes work any interface/class. Lambdas require effectively final captured variables; anonymous classes have own instance variables. Performance: lambdas optimized via invokedynamic bytecode, often faster than anonymous classes generating separate .class files. For simple functional operations, lambdas preferred for clarity. For complex implementations or non-functional scenarios, anonymous classes necessary. Recommendation: use lambdas whenever possible for clarity and conciseness; fall back to anonymous classes when needed.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Can lambda expressions access instance and static variables?
answers.answer: Yes, lambdas freely access instance and static variables without restrictions. Unlike local variables, no 'effectively final' requirement. Instance variables live in object with stable lifetime independent of method invocation. Static variables belong to class. When lambda accesses instance variable, it implicitly accesses this.variableName. 'This' in lambda refers to enclosing class instance. Lambdas can access and MODIFY instance variables and static variables. Accessing instance variables in lambdas can modify object state, violating functional programming purity principles. However, sometimes necessary, especially callbacks. Generally better passing mutable state explicitly as parameters rather than relying on captured instance state. Key difference from local variables: instance and static variables can be reassigned in lambdas without restriction, making them more flexible but potentially more dangerous due to side effects.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: Why can't we use lambda expressions without functional interface?
answers.answer: Lambda expressions cannot exist without functional interface target type. Lambda requires target type determining signature and contract. Lambda parameters, return type, and behavior must match abstract method of functional interface. Without target type, compiler cannot determine what method lambda implements, parameter types, return type, or checked exceptions. Compiler performs target type inference examining context (assignment, method parameter, return type). Without context, compiler cannot infer target type. Lambdas specifically designed for functional interfaces (single-method implementations). Interface with multiple abstract methods creates ambiguity—which method does lambda implement? Attempting to use lambda without functional interface target produces compile error: 'Lambda expressions are not supported'. This limitation is strength—makes code readable, focused, idiomatic. If implementing non-functional interface, use anonymous class instead.

answers.section: IMAGE 23: Java 8 Stream API & Functional Interface
answers.question: What is method reference? Why do we need it?
answers.answer: Method reference uses :: syntax referring to existing method by name rather than implementing method body. Compact alternative to lambda expressions. Four types: static method reference (ClassName::staticMethod), instance method reference (instance::instanceMethod), class instance method reference (ClassName::instanceMethod), constructor reference (ClassName::new). Method references improve readability—System.out::println clearer than x -> System.out.println(x). Reduce boilerplate—reference existing method rather than wrapping in lambda. Enable composition of existing methods without wrapper code (DRY principle). Improve performance—JVM optimizes more effectively than lambda expressions. Make functional pipelines practical and readable—stream.map(Integer::parseInt).filter(n -> n > 5).forEach(System.out::println) is clear and expressive. Method references essential for building functional pipelines where composing multiple operations.
